prompt:
Estás trabajando en la etapa t01 (etapa 1) de una tarea de programación acumulativa.

Esta es la primera etapa de la secuencia. /workspace contiene únicamente los materiales entregados para esta etapa. Implementa una base completa y mantenible que pueda extenderse en etapas posteriores.

El estado exacto recibido al comienzo de esta etapa está disponible en modo de solo lectura en /baseline. Puedes inspeccionarlo o ejecutar:

  diff -ruN /baseline /workspace

Implementa únicamente los requisitos descritos en los archivos de la etapa actual. Las etapas anteriores proporcionan contexto de implementación, pero no constituyen requisitos nuevos para esta etapa.

Puedes crear, editar o eliminar archivos dentro de /workspace. No modifiques /baseline.

Al terminar, deja instrucciones claras para ejecutar o revisar tu solución. No hagas preguntas de seguimiento. Adopta supuestos razonables y documéntalos.

turns.toolResult:
CC3501 - Tarea 1 - Otoño 2024

En esta tarea usted creará un programa que simule un pinball o flipper. La evaluación de
este programa considera tres aspectos: 1) diseño; 2) vistas y proyecciones; 3)
interactividad.

Diseño (3 puntos)
La cantidad de piezas en el diseño del flipper queda a su discreción, pero tiene por lo
menos:

●  Un tablero sobre el que se disponen los demás elementos.
●  Dos flippers que son las paletas que se activan mediante input y que golpean a la

pelota que se mueve por el tablero.

●  Los bordes que limitan por dónde se podrá mover la pelota (el diseño queda a su

discreción).

●  Por lo menos 2 elementos con los que interactúa la pelota. Hay distintos tipos de

interacción: chocar, rebotar, quedar atrapada y atravesar.

●  Por lo menos 2 elementos decorativos que son visibles pero no son parte de la

interacción con la pelota (por ej., en un flipper de Star Trek suele haber una nave
espacial encima del tablero).

Puede ver un ejemplo de las piezas de un flipper aquí:
(active los subtítulos).

Anatomy of a Pinball Machine

El puntaje de esta sección solo cuenta si los objetos son 3D:

●  tablero → 0.5
●  flipper → 0.5 c/u
●  bordes → 0.5
●  elementos extra → 0.25 c/u
●  elementos decorativos → 0.25 c/u.

La nota máxima es 3 puntos, incluso si tiene más elementos de los pedidos.

Los elementos que no sean decorativos deben ser de su autoría. Puede hacerlos en el
código o en Blender u otro software similar. Los elementos decorativos pueden ser
modelos 3D descargados de la red.

Nota I: Todo lo que grafique deben ser modelos 3D. Se sugiere que haga diseños 2D que
sean extendidos a 3D, como algunos de los elementos que se ven en la siguiente imagen:

 Nota II: No es necesario que haga una bola en esta tarea. Su incorporación en el flipper es
parte de la siguiente tarea. Sin embargo, debe implementar su tarea considerando que
tendrá que hacer ese añadido más adelante. Para ello, asuma que la bola se desplazará en
un mundo 2D, a pesar de que el despliegue gráfico es 3D (puede pensar como ejemplo un
juego de plataformas “visto de lado” como Super Mario que, incluso si se grafica en 3D, es
un mundo 2D).

Vistas y Proyecciones (1.5 puntos)
Usted debe implementar dos vistas distintas. Una es la vista superior del flipper, que
utilizará una proyección ortográfica, y la otra es la vida “de jugador(a)”, que utiliza
proyección en perspectiva. En ambas vistas procure que el tablero completo sea visible. El
programa cambiará de vista al presionar la tecla C.

Interactividad (1.5 puntos)
Usted podrá interactuar con los flippers a través de las teclas A y D. Al presionar A, el flipper
izquierdo se levantará; al presionar D, lo hará el derecho. La duración y amplitud del
movimiento la decide usted.

¿Cómo hacer la tarea?
Esta es una sugerencia de estrategia para hacerla. No es la única manera, ni
necesariamente la mejor, pero podría ayudarle a planificar la implementación. Siga los
siguientes pasos:

1.  Dibuje en un papel su diseño de flipper, de modo que conozca los tamaños y

extensión de tablero y piezas.

 2.  Tener una ventana de pyglet que dibuje un rectángulo y dos triángulos. Ese

rectángulo representará la superficie del flipper y los triángulos representarán las
paletas. Recuerde que su flipper tiene piezas estáticas y piezas móviles, quizás sea
bueno que tenga dos funciones, una que dibuje lo estático y otra lo dinámico.
3.  Implemente las dos vistas utilizando lo visto en la clase de vistas y proyecciones.

Puede hacerlo en el orden que prefiera. En su función de dibujo, al comienzo
debiese evaluar cuál es la vista activa, y configurar sus shaders para esa vista.
4.  Implemente la interactividad de las paletas utilizando transformaciones. Esto

requerirá que usted lleve control del tiempo de la aplicación, de modo que sepa
cuándo se activó la paleta. Por ejemplo, digamos que el movimiento de la paleta
dura 1 segundo. Si usted presiona A, comience a contar el tiempo que ha pasado en
la aplicación desde que se presionó la tecla. Ese tiempo t determinará el ángulo de
transformación de la paleta. Una vez que se llega a t = 1 segundo, la paleta
comienza a devolverse. Cuando vuelve a su punto de origen deja de estar en el
estado “moviéndose” y no se le aplica ninguna transformación (o bien se le aplica
una rotación de 0 grados, dependerá de cómo lo implemente).

Si usted hace lo anterior, tendrá un flipper mínimo y una tarea funcional que tiene un 4.
Luego de eso puede trabajar en el diseño y objetos del flipper y así hacerlo tan bonito o
psicodélico como usted desee para subir la nota.

Es importante que el flipper sea funcional porque será necesario para la siguiente tarea. Si
hace la tarea al revés, enfocándose en el diseño visual, también puede tener nota 4, pero
tendrá que ponerse al día con el resto de la funcionalidad para la tarea 2.

Ejecución
Suba los archivos tarea1.py, vertex_program.glsl y fragment_program.glsl a U-Cursos. Su
proyecto se ejecutará de la siguiente manera en la carpeta raíz del repositorio del curso:

$ conda activate grafica
$ python tarea1.py

Si utiliza modelos 3D o archivos con funciones auxiliares que no son parte del repositorio,
debe subir todo el código en un archivo .zip que se descomprimirá en la carpeta raíz.

Si el programa se cae o la ventana no despliega un flipper, la nota es 1.

Fecha de entrega
19 de abril de 2024, 23:59 Hrs. No entregue en el último minuto, pues debido a motivos de
carga es posible que U-Cursos no logre subir la tarea a tiempo.

 No habrá plazo adicional, ni para subir tareas que no se alcanzaron a subir al sistema
ni para dar el fin de semana. Por tanto, no deje la tarea ni su entrega para última hora1.

Por favor: ¡NO DEJE LA TAREA PARA
ÚLTIMA HORA!

1 Dato Rossa: de quienes han reprobado el curso en los últimos semestres, el motivo principal ha sido tareas y
no controles. Recuerde que tareas y controles se aprueban por separado.

content.text:
**Planning app development**

I need to build an app using Pyglet, and I’m thinking about incorporating a shader pipeline. I should include 3D objects like a board, two flippers, borders, two interactive elements, and two decorative pieces. The app requires two views: a top orthographic view and a player perspective, with controls assigned to C for switching and A/D for the flippers. This is just the first stage, so no ball is needed yet, but I want it designed for a 2D world.

**Building a PyGlet App**

I'm planning to create a Python app using PyGlet 2.0, focusing on windows and shaders. I might use a custom vertex list with a batch for efficiency. A simpler approach is to utilize the `ShaderProgram` and vertex lists for building shapes manually while loading the GLSL code. 

I could structure the program by creating a `Mesh` class that incorporates the vertex list and model matrix. I’ll use a common shader for position and color without needing normals. For the design, I envision a large flat cuboid board, trapezoid flippers, and thin cube walls for borders around the edges. I'll also include interactive elements like bumpers or pillars for collision interactions.

**Designing Interactive Elements**

I'm thinking about creating two tall decorative cylinders as non-interactive 3D objects. These could be stars, pyramids, or lights above the board, keeping the design simple with geometric shapes. 

For views, I need a top view using orthographic projection, positioning the camera above the board, looking down along the z-axis. Since the world is 2D on the x-y plane, I want to ensure the entire board is visible. I'll also consider an angled player view. Lastly, I need to animate the flippers using time-based state adjustments, anchoring the left flipper at the bottom left of the board.

**Animating Flippers**

I'm considering implementing the flippers to rotate around a hinge at one end. I’ll create a local transform matrix for this pivot point. When pressing A or D, I’ll set the start time and movement direction. The flippers will raise over 0.15 seconds and then return over 0.3 seconds. Since there's no ball involved, I can simply animate the flippers moving up and down. Each flipper might operate independently, activating on key press if they're not already active.

**Planning animation functions**

I'm considering how to handle the timing for my animation. It's probably easiest to use a basic time function, but with a pyglet app, I might want to utilize time.perf_counter instead. I need to calculate angles during the update and simplify it in the on_draw call. 

Maybe I should implement a `FlipperAnimation` class with properties like duration and max_angle, and set it up to allow repeated presses. I can compute elapsed time, making sure to create the necessary 3D models for the interactive elements too.

**Considering shader implementation**

I'm thinking about the challenges I'll face with shader code and projection matrices. It seems like I'll need to use `pyglet.graphics.shader.Shader` with GLSL 330 since OpenGL usually supports version 3.3. 

I might want to include both fragment and vertex shaders with color and check for a `mat4 mvp` uniform. For vertex lists, using `program.vertex_list_indexed` seems easier than `batch.add_indexed`—I'll need to confirm this with some API searches or examples. 

I should also consider whether to use `numpy` for matrices or stick with `pyglet.math` features, like `Mat4`. It looks like `Mat4` could simplify things with methods for projections and transformations.

from pyglet.math import Mat4, Vec3
print([m for m in dir(Mat4) if 'projection' in m.lower() or 'look' in m.lower()][:20])
print(Mat4)
PY

content.text:
**Designing custom shapes**

I’m considering creating some custom shapes, possibly writing helper functions. One could be `make_box(w,d,h,color,batch,program)` to generate a drawable object with vertices and triangles. For flippers, I might use a simpler cuboid shape, possibly with rounded ends. Two flippers could be boxes rotated at their bases, but since designing the shape isn’t critical, I might just enclose the board edges with four boxes and leave a gap at the bottom for the flippers. I guess I'll establish the bounds for now!

**Clarifying the board layout**

I’m thinking about creating bottom walls on the left and right sides, leaving a central gap for where the ball will exit later. Since there’s no ball yet, it should keep the board visible. For my top view, I’m realizing that if the board lies on the x/y plane, I’ll need to consider viewing from the +z down direction, making depth in the y-axis. The flippers will need to rotate in the x/y plane around the z-axis, just like in actual pinball!

**Defining the player perspective**

This feels more consistent now! The player's perspective will show the board slanted in 3D since it’s not viewed from above. The flippers will indeed rotate around the z-axis. So, I think of the board as a flat cuboid lying on the x/y plane with a thickness in the z-axis. For the borders, I'll extrude walls along the z-axis around the board edges, and the bumpers can be cylinders also extruded along z. Decorative elements could be additional cylinders or pyramids!

**Designing interactive elements**

I’m thinking about creating a top view of the board with a camera positioned above it. Since the board is a 2D plane on the x/y axes, an orthographic view makes sense. I’ll need to implement interactive elements, like collision objects such as bumpers, and perhaps a tunnel or target. Since we don’t have a ball yet, I’ll focus on making these elements visually suggest interaction types to meet the requirements. The borders already interact, which is a start!

**Clarifying interactive elements**

The prompt specifies needing at least two elements for the ball to interact with, and there are different types of interactions: bounce, get trapped, or pass through. I’m wondering if the borders count, but to be safe, I’ll add a bumper and a tunnel as extra elements. I can create two bumpers, positioned in the upper corners, and perhaps a decorative gate or ramp. While the bumpers handle interaction, I could also include some decorative cones or towers for visual flair!

**Adding decorative elements**

I’m thinking about incorporating visible yet noninteractive objects, like lights and antennas, to enhance the scene. I could add two decorative pillars with colorful domes or maybe a simple rocket design. Since we’re working in 3D, I can create a pair of tall prisms or cones above the top wall, giving it a sci-fi vibe. To keep everything organized, I should use a scene graph or object list, creating a `SceneObject` with a transform and mesh for easier management!

**Considering pyglet rendering**

I'm thinking about how to manage the viewport and camera matrices using pyglet. It might be good to use a `Batch` for individual object rendering, which could involve setting uniforms and calling `vertex_list.draw(GL_TRIANGLES)`. For organization, maybe I could have a `Mesh` class for the vertex list and a `Node` class for transforms and meshes. As I write code in `tarea1.py`, I should ensure clarity in instructions, perhaps through comments. I also need to confirm shader uniform setup in pyglet 2.0.

**Prototyping geometry functions**

It seems I should set `color=('f', colors)` with a float list ranging from 0 to 1. I also need to enable `GL_DEPTH_TEST` and set a clear color. Handling resizing will be important too. For the scene, I’m considering using the `Window` class with `on_draw`, `on_key_press`, `on_resize`, and `update` methods. Prototyping simple shapes might involve helper functions like `create_box` and `create_cylinder`. I’ll need to define base geometry functions that generate vertices and indices effectively.

**Designing a 3D object**

I’m thinking about creating a cylindrical shape with a position centered at thickness divided by two. This makes sense for a board on the surface. I’ll use functions like `make_cylinder` for side faces and caps, which places vertices around a circle at specified heights. For decorative elements, I’m considering either a pyramid or a cone, possibly shaped like a flipper with a box and cylinder. I want to keep it streamlined, ensuring it maintains the stage design and OpenGL coordinates. I'll set up the camera perspective too!

**Creating flippers design**

I need to make flippers that pivot around the z-axis. The left flipper will pivot at (-5.5, -10) with an upward angle, while the right one will pivot at (5.5, -10) with an opposite angle. I will build the geometry in local coordinates with the pivot at one end. The local base for the left flipper can point along the +x axis, and then I'll rotate around the z-axis. I need to establish the transform carefully, considering mesh origin and local vertex coordinates for proper alignment.

**Designing the flipper mesh**

I'm thinking that the function `make_flipper_mesh(length, width, height)` will create cuboid vertices ranging from 0 to L along the x-axis, with the y-coordinates set to ±width/2 and z from 0 to h. After generating the vertices, I need to translate them to the pivot point. The rotation around the z-axis will occur at the pivot, starting from the origin. This approach seems solid for constructing the flipper geometry!

**Implementing Scene class**

I think I need to create a `Scene` class that includes a `draw(view, proj)` method and an `update` function. Each object in the scene will also have its own `draw` method, which sets the `mvp` matrix. This way, the program can be more structured since the functionality seems to be the same across different objects. It's about organizing the code better while ensuring everything works together seamlessly!

**Inspecting methods**

I'm wondering if I should explore those `()` methods or if I could alternatively use `with self.program:`. It might be good to verify this, so I'll check quickly with a mini script, but I'm not sure if that's necessary right now. Instead, maybe I can just inspect the directory to see what options are available. Let’s see how that goes!

content.text:
**Sketching mesh generation**

I need to ensure that shader variable names match attribute names. It’s also important to create mesh generation functions. For example, I’m defining a `Mesh` class with a method to draw the vertex list as triangles. 

I’m working on the `make_box` function to create vertices and indices for a box shape. Additionally, I’ll consider colors for the vertices and explore creating an octagonal prism with a defined number of segments, ensuring a counter-clockwise order for the indices.

**Exploring decorative shapes**

Since culling is disabled, orientation isn't a big issue for my design. I'm thinking about using a cone or dome for decor, but I can opt for cylinder lights with a small sphere. There's also the option of a tall tower topped with a pyramid. I could create a `make_cone` function to build an extruded polygon with a top point. 

Alternatively, creating two neon towers as cylinders with cones on top sounds appealing. I could also consider a `make_prism_polygon(points, height)` function for more complex shapes, but simple designs like boxes and cylinders might be sufficient.

**Designing extruded shapes**

I'm considering the design for a flipper, which should be more paddle-like, or maybe create a tapered option that’s a slender box. Using a 2D trapezoid shape extruded into 3D seems like the way to go. I think I’ll use polygon points like (0,-0.45), (1.7,-0.4), (2.2,0), (1.7,0.4), and (0,0.45) to shape that paddle. I’m also thinking about making mirrored versions for the left and right ends, which could be really efficient! I’ll implement this generic extruded polygon mesh.

**Designing a mesh structure**

I'm thinking that I should probably center the base at z=0 and extrude from there to a specified height. This could work perfectly for a board surface at z=0. For the top and bottom of the flippers, I can triangulate using a fan shape. The board could be a simple cuboid or an extruded polygon rectangle with a slight taper for the flipper. I’ll also need to generate side faces for each edge and use `make_extruded_polygon_mesh` for those. Additionally, I could use the same logic to create cylinders and maybe even some decorative star-shaped polygons too!

**Creating decorative elements**

I'm considering making two decorative extruded polygons with different silhouettes using `extrude_polygon`. They could be scaled or stretched to add some variety, perhaps even resembling a "rocket" shape with six sides. It seems like decorative elements don’t need to be overly complex or interactive. I could also design a simple "sign" using a thin box combined with a dome shape. That might give it a nice touch without overcomplicating things!

**Designing scene elements**

I’m thinking about how a single element can have multiple nodes, but the requirement counts elements visually, not in implementation details. I might create a "tower" with a stem box and a top cap cylinder. If I have two towers, each made from two meshes, it could increase my code, but that’s manageable. I also want to ensure no external files are needed, just code. For scene elements, I’m considering the dimensions of the board and borders.

**Creating flippers and wall segments**

I’m planning to create two small wall segments. For the flippers, the left flipper base will be at coordinates (-5.5, -14.0, 0.45). I want the local polygon to span from x=0 to 6, with a width of about 1.2 and a height from 0 to 0.5. The base angle might be 25 degrees upward, but I think I should angle the left flipper to the right and up, reflecting how flippers are positioned in pinball.

**Designing game elements**

I'm thinking about adding a central target at (0,3) as a thin cylinder or ring, but the requirement is to only include two elements. Ideally, two bumpers would work since they "rebound." For decorative elements, I might put sci-fi pylons at the top corners outside the board, maybe at (-9,16) and (9,16), with a cylinder base and cone top. I could also consider a logo panel. However, I need to avoid clutter and ensure maintainability with separate classes for different components in a single file. It's crucial to think about supporting a headless environment too.

**Evaluating testing options**

I'm considering whether they should launch a window for evaluation. However, I can't test the GUI here, which complicates things. I could compile the code to make sure there are no syntax errors. Using pyglet in headless mode could be an option, but it might not be necessary. Running non-window tests for geometry seems feasible. I think it's sufficient to use `python -m py_compile tarea1.py`, and I might also explore setting `pyglet.options['headless']`.

**Exploring rendering with pyglet**

For standard use, I think it’s good to have the `Mesh` store `vertex_list`, `mode`, and `num_indices`. The `draw(GL_TRIANGLES)` method should work fine. I’m a bit concerned about the `pyglet.graphics.shader.ShaderProgram` with matrices, specifically how to set a uniform for `program["mvp"]`. 

Could `Mat4` be accepted? If I convert it to a tuple of 16 floats, will that work? It looks like testing might be the best way to confirm this!

content.text:
**Considering implementation details**

I'm thinking about using `pyglet.window.key` for key constants, which seems helpful. Creating keyboard hints in the window title could be nice, but maybe not necessary. I’ll try to avoid using any assets to keep things simple. I should be careful about potential issues with `vertex_list_indexed` and shader attribute names, making sure they match up properly. If I'm using color arrays, I should consider their components, and I'll stick with a flat color approach without normals. Also, I need to ensure support for resizing.

**Planning update scheduling**

I need to think about scheduling updates, possibly with something like `pyglet.clock.schedule_interval(self.update, 1/60)`, or by utilizing the event loop. Since the angle is computed from `perf_counter`, perhaps I should redraw by marking `self.invalid`. I wonder if `on_draw` alone would be sufficient, as pyglet might not redraw continuously without `schedule_interval`. I’ll definitely need to call `self.window.invalid = True` to ensure it updates. Overall, scheduling the update at 1/60 seems safest, so I can handle redraws efficiently.

**Exploring window methods**

I'm considering whether I should use `self.window.invalid = True`. I wonder if it's necessary, since pyglet redraws windows on each loop iteration if scheduled functions are present. The app loop dispatches window events and processes draw events when windows are invalid. To animate, often an interval is scheduled, and one calls `window.invalid = True`. I'm also checking if `window.invalidate()` exists, since `window.invalid = True` seems to work in pyglet 2. Time to inspect further!

arguments.command:
python - <<'PY'
import pyglet

I'm considering a field of view of 55 and positioning the camera at (0, -42, 22) with a target at (0, 0, 2). I need to ensure that the entire board is visible, so maybe I should set the far plane to 150. Since the board is in the x-y plane and the camera is from a negative y position, I might need to adjust the camera back and up for better visibility. 

Also, I'm thinking about potential z-fighting issues from overlapping board surfaces starting at z=0. It might help to offset borders slightly above the board to resolve this. I'll use model transforms to place them correctly.

**Evaluating board design**

I'm considering the board thickness, which is good, with everything extruding upward from z=0. I wonder if a board shadow is necessary, but maybe I can go with a darker board color. I think about adding lights or targets at heights above the board, and it seems fine to place elements at the thickness. 

An inner lane or ramp could complicate the player's view, so I might skip that. The towers and bumpers meet the decorative and interactive requirements. I need to ensure to create clear instructions after editing.

**Crafting code for pinball project**

I need to include a note that the final answer should run `python tarea1.py`, and I should clarify the assumption about the scene using the x-y plane with the z-axis up. Writing the code from scratch with proper imports and a docstring might be the best approach. I'm considering using `from __future__ import annotations` and dataclasses. 

I might need to handle importing pyglet in headless mode, which usually works fine, and I want to ensure the window is created in the main section. I’ll outline the code, starting with the docstring indicating that this is for the pinball project.

**Setting up the project structure**

I've established the assumption that the world will be in the x-y plane, with z representing height. I’ll start by importing the necessary libraries, including dataclass, math functions, time, and pyglet along with GL functionalities like `glEnable`, `GL_DEPTH_TEST`, and `GL_CULL_FACE`.

I’m unsure if I need to include `GL_CULL_FACE`, so I might leave it out for now. Overall, I want to ensure I have everything set up correctly before diving deeper into the project’s implementation.

**Building extruded polygons**

I'm working on a function to build an extruded polygon, taking parameters like program, points, height, and color. I've considered the vertices and possibly reversing the top face order for better rendering. I'm defining indices for the polygon's sides and faces, ensuring they remain convex. I plan to create box points and possibly use regular polygons in the future. The idea of tapering flipper points into a more flipper-like shape is intriguing!

**Evaluating angle rotations**

I'm thinking about the geometry of flippers. The left flipper extends in the positive x direction, and I believe an angle of 25° is correct. For the right flipper, the geometry mirrors, extending in the negative x direction. If I rotate by 155°, the shape roughly points down-right, which is not what I want. So, maybe I should adjust the angle to around 335° or -25° to ensure it points towards the center, possibly around 155° from the positive x-axis.

**Clarifying rotation and reflection**

I'm thinking about how to adjust the geometry for orientation. If the original shape points left before any rotation, to orient it at 155° from the positive x-axis, I should first rotate the local x-axis to about 25°. Then, after mirroring, it should point to 155°. This happens because scaling by -1 on the x-axis flips the direction. I should derive the formula: for a point on the local x-axis, after applying S(-1, 1), the vector (1, 0) becomes (-1, 0).

**Considering flipper animation**

I need to convert degrees to radians for `FlipperAnimation.angle`. I think unifying the flipper drawing with a model function for left or right transforms could be beneficial. I could create a `Flipper` dataclass with attributes like `pivot`, `rest_degrees`, `direction`, and `animation`. I plan to express the angle in terms of `rest` and `animation.current_offset_deg`, depending on the direction. The animation offsets should align with the signs for a simpler approach. Defining the rest angles as 25° for left and -25° for right seems straightforward!

**Finalizing angle calculations**

For the right direction, I’ve figured out that the angle is calculated as -25 minus the offset. After applying a mirror orientation, the formula becomes angle + 180, which results in 155 minus the offset. This means the angle transitions from 155 to 115 as expected. Everything seems to be lining up perfectly, and I feel confident in this approach for the flipper's angle calculations!

**Considering potential issues**

I’m pondering whether the keys should be uppercase or lowercase. It seems like `key.A` should handle A, irrespective of that. I might not need to print instructions. There's a potential issue with `program.vertex_list_indexed` requiring `indices` as an array. For `position=('f', positions)` and `color=('f', colors)`, they might need to be tuples or lists. I’m contemplating creating a `make_mesh` function and `build_extruded_polygon_mesh`. Also, can headless tests check for file existence and code imports? It’s tricky thinking about how to ensure robust code in environments without displays.

**Considering object models for flippers**

I’m thinking about how flipper models generate from the current angle, which depends on time. Maybe the object model could be a callable function for calculating per frame. For `Scene.draw`, if the object is callable, I'll use `mat = obj.model()`. I should check if it’s callable using `callable()`. In `build_objects`, I'll create the board mesh and walls, placing them appropriately in the scene. I also want to consider bumpers and decorative towers to enhance the visual elements.

**Thinking about creating a cone mesh**

I’m considering making a `make_cone` mesh to serve as the top part of an object. This could add a nice visual detail. I’ll need to think about the dimensions and shape to ensure it fits well within the overall design. Maybe I should explore how to implement this efficiently within the existing mesh system. I want to make sure everything looks cohesive and polished when it's done.

**Considering mesh implementations**

I'm thinking about creating a `make_cone_mesh` that uses base polygon points from `regular_polygon(sides, radius)`. For the `make_tapered_flipper_mesh`, I could extrude the same polygon points. It might be easier to just use a `capsule`. I’ll implement functions like `build_extruded_polygon_mesh`, `build_cone_mesh`, and `build_box_mesh` to simplify the process. In the scene building, I need to set up the walls with correct transformations and dimensions, ensuring everything fits together nicely.

**Calculating game board layout**

I'm rethinking the bottom wall segments, placing them on the sides of the flippers. I'm setting the gap width to 8.0 in the center, and each segment width is calculated based on the board width. For a board width of 24 and a gap of 7.0, each segment width comes to 8.5. I can position bumpers, possibly cylinders, with specified dimensions and colors.

Decorative towers can be positioned for visual appeal and include specific measurements. Finally, I'm defining the flippers' size and shape for this layout.

**Defining flipper mechanics**

I'm setting the pivot points for the flippers at x positions ±6 and y at -14. The flippers need to sit just above the lower wall segments, which seems ideal. I’m thinking about positioning the left flipper with its pivot at around -5.8 and an angle of 25°. If the length is 5.2, the tip would end at approximately x = -1.1 and y = -11.8, starting from y = -14.

For the right flipper, I’ll mirror its position, setting the pivot at x = 5.8 with an angle of 155°. This setup ensures both flippers reach the center and have the necessary positioning relative to the bottom gap.

**Examining shader development**

I'm considering how to implement key interactions in my project. The idea of using `key.C` to toggle the view and potentially print the current view is interesting, but not required. I might handle cases for both upper and lower case with `on_key_press`. 

I'm thinking about shader file reading and maybe needing to write the shader files before running the Python program. I've started crafting the `vertex_program.glsl`, but I need to consider adding line breaks and some configurations like disabling face culling for performance.

**Optimizing shader usage**

I'm considering whether to use `glEnable(GL_BLEND)`, but it might not be necessary. It seems safe to set `self.program.use()` before drawing and `self.program.stop()` after. I’ll review the `ShaderProgram` methods for confirmation.

In the `on_draw` function, I'll ensure I'm assigning `self.program["mvp"]` to the correct values for each object. For better performance, I could compute the view-projection matrix only once. Also, I need to verify that I'm using the exact filenames for upload as specified, like `tarea1.py`, `vertex_program.glsl`, and `fragment_program.glsl`. Keeping it straightforward is key!

**Writing tarefa1.py**

I need to write tarefa1.py, and it's important that I avoid any syntax errors. I'll make sure to draft with care, taking my time to check everything as I go. It's easy to overlook small details that can lead to issues later. I want to ensure that the code functions correctly and is clean. So, I'll be methodical about it and give it my full attention!

**Fixing rendering issues**

I need to address the glDisable imported constant issue. I used 0x0B44 instead of GL_CULL_FACE, which isn't ideal. I should import GL_CULL_FACE or use the symbolic constant instead of the numeric one. I'll check if GL_CULL_FACE is available, as that's likely the better option. Also, there's the potential for an issue with using the same flipper_mesh instance twice. Lastly, I'll have to ensure my imports are correct for any required functionalities. Ready for a patch!

**Reviewing rendering and animation logic**

I’m identifying potential issues with the `mesh.draw()` method while a shader program is active, but that seems okay for now. The `program.vertex_list_indexed` using `position=("f", vertex_positions)` may just return a list of float components, which is good. There’s also a concern about `self.invalid = True` in the update — that may be sufficient. 

I’m also contemplating the need for `glFlush` and how `on_draw` schedules might be affected by `window.invalid`. Currently, `scene.update_time()` ties animation to the draw event, but that may work since invalid is set each tick. Finally, the `FlipperAnimation.angle` needs adjustment for holding at max duration, but it’s all good for now!

**Examining animation trigger issues**

I’m noticing a potential issue with the `trigger()` function, especially when the animation is active and might be resetting unexpectedly. This could affect the flow of the animation or unintended behaviors. I'll need to investigate this further to ensure that the animation runs smoothly without any interruptions. It’s good to catch these potential problems early, as addressing them now could save me from bigger headaches down the line. Let's dig deeper into this!

**Inspecting camera settings**

I’m considering potential adjustments with a field of view (FOV) of 55 and the camera positioned at -42, 24, which seems to work. If necessary, I might tweak it, but for now, it looks good. I should also check the top view, which uses orthographic bounds of ±17 and ±23, ensuring decorations at y 15.5 fit properly. I think it’s a good idea to inspect the syntax by running a compilation. Let's go ahead with that!

**Considering projections and aspects**

I'm thinking about whether to use `pyglet.app.run()` if no display leads to no window. I need to patch the `glDisable` constant. It’s also interesting to consider the board's top view projection and its aspect ratio. Since the orthographic view is independent of the aspect, it could stretch with a wide window. I want to ensure the entire board is visible, but can I allow for some distortion? It seems better to use aspect-correct bounds for the top view orthographic projection. I should compute the orthographic view to keep proportions aligned with the aspect ratio. Based on the board’s extents and margins, I could adjust the projection to fit the window ratio. I might set the width to match the aspect, but that could make the board smaller. Preserving the complete board visibility while fitting the viewport correctly sounds like the way to go!

**Adjusting dimensions for visibility**

If the height is 46, then to match the aspect ratio, the width should be 69. That’s pretty wide, but the board is only 24 wide, so it should still be visible. I’m wondering if there might be too much horizontal margin, but it seems manageable. Keeping the board visible is the priority, so I could set `ortho_half_height` to half the board length plus the margin, making it 23. That could work out!

turns.toolResult:
"""CC3501 - Tarea 1

Base completa del flipper/pinball para la etapa 1.
Supuesto de trabajo: el mundo del juego vive en el plano x-y y el eje z es la altura.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from math import cos, pi, radians, sin
from pathlib import Path
from time import perf_counter
from typing import Callable, Iterable

import pyglet
from pyglet.gl import GL_CULL_FACE, GL_DEPTH_TEST, GL_TRIANGLES, glClearColor, glDisable, glEnable
from pyglet.graphics.shader import Shader, ShaderProgram
from pyglet.math import Mat4, Vec3
from pyglet.window import Window, key


ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent
VERTEX_SHADER_PATH = ROOT / "vertex_program.glsl"
FRAGMENT_SHADER_PATH = ROOT / "fragment_program.glsl"


# Escala general de la mesa.
BOARD_WIDTH = 24.0
BOARD_LENGTH = 36.0
BOARD_THICKNESS = 0.45
WALL_HEIGHT = 2.2
WALL_THICKNESS = 0.7

FLIPPER_LENGTH = 5.7
FLIPPER_WIDTH = 1.25
FLIPPER_THICKNESS = 0.35
FLIPPER_LIFT_DEG = 42.0
FLIPPER_DURATION = 0.42
FLIPPER_UP_TIME = 0.13

TOP_VIEW_MARGIN = 5.0


# ---------- Matrices helpers ----------

def translate(x: float, y: float, z: float) -> Mat4:
    return Mat4.from_translation(Vec3(x, y, z))


def rotate_z(angle_radians: float) -> Mat4:
    return Mat4.from_rotation(angle_radians, Vec3(0.0, 0.0, 1.0))


def scale(x: float, y: float, z: float) -> Mat4:
    return Mat4.from_scale(Vec3(x, y, z))


# ---------- Geometry ----------


def regular_polygon(sides: int, radius: float, phase: float = 0.0) -> list[tuple[float, float]]:
    points: list[tuple[float, float]] = []
    for i in range(sides):
        angle = phase + 2.0 * pi * i / sides
        points.append((radius * cos(angle), radius * sin(angle)))
    return points


@dataclass(slots=True)
class Mesh:
    vertex_list: object

    def draw(self) -> None:
        self.vertex_list.draw(GL_TRIANGLES)


@dataclass(slots=True)
class Renderable:
    mesh: Mesh
    model: Callable[[], Mat4] | Mat4

    def model_matrix(self) -> Mat4:
        return self.model() if callable(self.model) else self.model


@dataclass(slots=True)
class FlipperAnimation:
    rest_angle_deg: float
    direction: float
    start_time: float | None = None

    def trigger(self, now: float) -> None:
        self.start_time = now

    def angle(self, now: float) -> float:
        if self.start_time is None:
            return radians(self.rest_angle_deg)

        elapsed = now - self.start_time
        if elapsed >= FLIPPER_DURATION:
            self.start_time = None
            return radians(self.rest_angle_deg)

        if elapsed <= FLIPPER_UP_TIME:
            offset = FLIPPER_LIFT_DEG * (elapsed / FLIPPER_UP_TIME)
        else:
            remaining = FLIPPER_DURATION - FLIPPER_UP_TIME
            offset = FLIPPER_LIFT_DEG * (1.0 - (elapsed - FLIPPER_UP_TIME) / remaining)

        return radians(self.rest_angle_deg + self.direction * offset)


class GeometryFactory:
    def __init__(self, program: ShaderProgram) -> None:
        self.program = program

    def extruded_polygon(
        self,
        points: Iterable[tuple[float, float]],
        height: float,
        color: tuple[float, float, float],
    ) -> Mesh:
        points = list(points)
        if len(points) < 3:
            raise ValueError("An extruded polygon needs at least 3 points")

        vertex_positions: list[float] = []
        for z in (0.0, height):
            for x, y in points:
                vertex_positions.extend([x, y, z])

        bottom_offset = 0
        top_offset = len(points)
        indices: list[int] = []

        # Bottom face.
        for i in range(1, len(points) - 1):
            indices.extend([bottom_offset, bottom_offset + i, bottom_offset + i + 1])

        # Top face.
        for i in range(1, len(points) - 1):
            indices.extend([top_offset, top_offset + i + 1, top_offset + i])

        # Side faces.
        for i in range(len(points)):
            j = (i + 1) % len(points)
            indices.extend(
                [
                    bottom_offset + i,
                    bottom_offset + j,
                    top_offset + j,
                    bottom_offset + i,
                    top_offset + j,
                    top_offset + i,
                ]
            )

        vertex_colors = list(color) * (len(points) * 2)
        vertex_list = self.program.vertex_list_indexed(
            len(points) * 2,
            GL_TRIANGLES,
            indices,
            position=("f", vertex_positions),
            color=("f", vertex_colors),
        )
        return Mesh(vertex_list)

    def box(self, width: float, depth: float, height: float, color: tuple[float, float, float]) -> Mesh:
        hw = width / 2.0
        hd = depth / 2.0
        return self.extruded_polygon(
            [(-hw, -hd), (hw, -hd), (hw, hd), (-hw, hd)],
            height,
            color,
        )

    def cylinder(
        self,
        radius: float,
        height: float,
        color: tuple[float, float, float],
        sides: int = 24,
    ) -> Mesh:
        return self.extruded_polygon(regular_polygon(sides, radius), height, color)

    def cone(
        self,
        radius: float,
        height: float,
        color: tuple[float, float, float],
        sides: int = 18,
    ) -> Mesh:
        base = regular_polygon(sides, radius)
        vertex_positions: list[float] = []
        for x, y in base:
            vertex_positions.extend([x, y, 0.0])
        vertex_positions.extend([0.0, 0.0, height])

        indices: list[int] = []
        apex = len(base)
        for i in range(1, len(base) - 1):
            indices.extend([0, i + 1, i])
        for i in range(len(base)):
            j = (i + 1) % len(base)
            indices.extend([i, j, apex])

        vertex_colors = list(color) * (len(base) + 1)
        vertex_list = self.program.vertex_list_indexed(
            len(base) + 1,
            GL_TRIANGLES,
            indices,
            position=("f", vertex_positions),
            color=("f", vertex_colors),
        )
        return Mesh(vertex_list)

    def flipper(self, color: tuple[float, float, float]) -> Mesh:
        # Una silueta ligeramente cónica para que se vea más parecida a una paleta.
        points = [
            (0.0, -FLIPPER_WIDTH / 2.0),
            (FLIPPER_LENGTH * 0.68, -FLIPPER_WIDTH * 0.42),
            (FLIPPER_LENGTH, 0.0),
            (FLIPPER_LENGTH * 0.68, FLIPPER_WIDTH * 0.42),
            (0.0, FLIPPER_WIDTH / 2.0),
        ]
        return self.extruded_polygon(points, FLIPPER_THICKNESS, color)


class PinballScene:
    def __init__(self, program: ShaderProgram) -> None:
        self.program = program
        self.factory = GeometryFactory(program)
        self.renderables: list[Renderable] = []
        self.view_mode = "top"
        self.now = perf_counter()

        self.left_flipper = FlipperAnimation(rest_angle_deg=25.0, direction=1.0)
        self.right_flipper = FlipperAnimation(rest_angle_deg=-25.0, direction=-1.0)

        self._build_scene()

    def _build_scene(self) -> None:
        # Base tablero.
        board_mesh = self.factory.box(BOARD_WIDTH, BOARD_LENGTH, BOARD_THICKNESS, (0.16, 0.22, 0.19))
        self.renderables.append(Renderable(board_mesh, translate(0.0, 0.0, 0.0)))

        # Bordes.
        border_color = (0.63, 0.44, 0.21)
        self.renderables.extend(
            [
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.box(WALL_THICKNESS, BOARD_LENGTH, WALL_HEIGHT, border_color),
                    translate(-BOARD_WIDTH / 2.0 + WALL_THICKNESS / 2.0, 0.0, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                ),
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.box(WALL_THICKNESS, BOARD_LENGTH, WALL_HEIGHT, border_color),
                    translate(BOARD_WIDTH / 2.0 - WALL_THICKNESS / 2.0, 0.0, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                ),
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.box(BOARD_WIDTH, WALL_THICKNESS, WALL_HEIGHT, border_color),
                    translate(0.0, BOARD_LENGTH / 2.0 - WALL_THICKNESS / 2.0, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                ),
            ]
        )

        bottom_y = -BOARD_LENGTH / 2.0 + WALL_THICKNESS / 2.0
        gap = 7.4
        left_bottom_width = (BOARD_WIDTH - gap) / 2.0
        right_bottom_width = left_bottom_width
        self.renderables.extend(
            [
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.box(left_bottom_width, WALL_THICKNESS, WALL_HEIGHT, border_color),
                    translate(-gap / 2.0 - left_bottom_width / 2.0, bottom_y, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                ),
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.box(right_bottom_width, WALL_THICKNESS, WALL_HEIGHT, border_color),
                    translate(gap / 2.0 + right_bottom_width / 2.0, bottom_y, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                ),
            ]
        )

        # Elementos con los que interactúa la pelota.
        self.renderables.extend(
            [
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.cylinder(1.15, 1.15, (0.92, 0.78, 0.18), sides=22),
                    translate(-6.5, 8.5, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                ),
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.cylinder(1.0, 1.4, (0.2, 0.66, 0.9), sides=20),
                    translate(6.3, 10.2, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                ),
            ]
        )

        # Decoración.
        decoration_positions = [(-9.5, 15.5), (9.5, 15.5)]
        decoration_colors = [
            ((0.75, 0.18, 0.68), (0.95, 0.4, 0.88)),
            ((0.2, 0.8, 0.55), (0.55, 0.98, 0.75)),
        ]
        for (x, y), (stem_color, cap_color) in zip(decoration_positions, decoration_colors, strict=True):
            self.renderables.append(
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.cylinder(0.42, 2.2, stem_color, sides=16),
                    translate(x, y, BOARD_THICKNESS),
                )
            )
            self.renderables.append(
                Renderable(
                    self.factory.cone(0.88, 1.2, cap_color, sides=16),
                    translate(x, y, BOARD_THICKNESS + 2.2),
                )
            )

        # Flippers.
        flipper_mesh = self.factory.flipper((0.88, 0.31, 0.25))
        self.renderables.append(
            Renderable(
                flipper_mesh,
                lambda: translate(-5.8, -14.0, BOARD_THICKNESS)
                * rotate_z(self.left_flipper.angle(self.now))
                * scale(1.0, 1.0, 1.0),
            )
        )
        self.renderables.append(
            Renderable(
                flipper_mesh,
                lambda: translate(5.8, -14.0, BOARD_THICKNESS)
                * rotate_z(self.right_flipper.angle(self.now))
                * scale(-1.0, 1.0, 1.0),
            )
        )

    def switch_view(self) -> None:
        self.view_mode = "player" if self.view_mode == "top" else "top"

    def trigger_left_flipper(self, now: float) -> None:
        self.left_flipper.trigger(now)

    def trigger_right_flipper(self, now: float) -> None:
        self.right_flipper.trigger(now)

    def update_time(self) -> None:
        self.now = perf_counter()

    def camera_matrices(self, aspect: float) -> tuple[Mat4, Mat4]:
        if self.view_mode == "top":
            margin = TOP_VIEW_MARGIN
            half_height = BOARD_LENGTH / 2.0 + margin
            half_width = max(BOARD_WIDTH / 2.0 + margin, half_height * aspect)
            projection = Mat4.orthogonal_projection(
                -half_width,
                half_width,
                -half_height,
                half_height,
                -20.0,
                40.0,
            )
            view = Mat4.look_at(
                Vec3(0.0, 0.0, 24.0),
                Vec3(0.0, 0.0, 0.0),
                Vec3(0.0, 1.0, 0.0),
            )
        else:
            projection = Mat4.perspective_projection(aspect, 0.1, 150.0, 55.0)
            view = Mat4.look_at(
                Vec3(0.0, -42.0, 24.0),
                Vec3(0.0, 0.0, 2.0),
                Vec3(0.0, 0.0, 1.0),
            )
        return projection, view

    def draw(self, aspect: float) -> None:
        projection, view = self.camera_matrices(aspect)
        vp = projection * view
        self.program.use()
        try:
            for renderable in self.renderables:
                self.program["mvp"] = tuple(vp * renderable.model_matrix())
                renderable.mesh.draw()
        finally:
            self.program.stop()


class PinballWindow(Window):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__(
            width=1200,
            height=800,
            caption="CC3501 - Tarea 1",
            resizable=True,
            vsync=True,
        )
        self._setup_gl()
        self.program = self._load_program()
        self.scene = PinballScene(self.program)
        self.aspect = self.width / max(1, self.height)
        self.invalid = True

content.text:
Listo.

Archivos creados:
- `tarea1.py`
- `vertex_program.glsl`
- `fragment_program.glsl`

Supuesto documentado: el tablero vive en el plano `x-y` y `z` es la altura.

Cómo ejecutar:
```bash
python tarea1.py
```

Controles:
- `A`: flipper izquierdo
- `D`: flipper derecho
- `C`: cambiar vista (superior / jugador)
